Low Conviction Ideas

Quantitative Research

Quant Models

The quantitative work behind the qualitative thesis. Simulation, technical, and statistical models used to pressure test position sizing and timing.

Reverse DCF & Expectations Analysis

Professional investors spend a lot of time asking one question: what does today's price already assume? This flips the DCF around. The market price is taken as given, and the model solves backward for the revenue growth the market must be pricing in.

This part of the site comes from a different piece of my background than the equity work above. Less about finding good businesses, more about measuring how much you could lose. Market risk, counterparty risk, and liquidity risk are genuinely different problems, and I'd rather treat them as separate questions than flatten them into a single number. I also don't trust a closed-form price until it's been checked against a full simulation, and correlations that hold up in calm markets tend to be the first ones to break when it actually matters.

Risk & Simulation Models

Monte Carlo work for counterparty and market risk. Exposure simulation, factor-based VaR with regime switching, marginal risk attribution.

PFE / EE on a 5Y USD Interest Rate Swap
Vasicek short-rate Monte Carlo, 50,000 paths on a monthly grid, marking a payer swap to market at every future date with closed-form bond pricing. Gives the full PFE and EE profile over the trade's life, plus EPE and EEPE.
Max PFE 95%: $1.39M @ 1.58y EPE: $144K EEPE: $261K 50,000 paths
DOWNLOAD .PY DOWNLOAD CHART
Multivariate Monte Carlo VaR: CATL (300750.SZ)
100,000-path simulation across four correlated risk factors (lithium, cobalt, oil, US-China tariff risk) with regime-switching correlation (80% normal / 20% stress) and Student-t fat tails. Outputs VaR and CVaR at 95%/99% across 3M/6M/12M horizons, with marginal VaR attribution by factor.
12M VaR 95%: -4.0% 12M CVaR 95%: -6.1% Lithium: 60.7% of VaR 100,000 paths
DOWNLOAD .PY DOWNLOAD CHART
Multivariate Monte Carlo VaR: Recruit Holdings (6098.T)
Same regime-switching, fat-tailed Monte Carlo framework applied to Recruit, factored on USD/JPY, US labor market conditions, AI disruption risk to staffing, and Japan SaaS multiples. VaR/CVaR across three horizons with marginal attribution by factor.
12M VaR 95%: -8.6% 12M CVaR 95%: -10.4% US Labor: 48.8% of VaR 100,000 paths
DOWNLOAD .PY DOWNLOAD CHART

Thumbnails and downloads are embedded directly in this page. Click any thumbnail to view full-size, or download the source .py to run the simulation yourself.

Curve Construction & Multi-Product Exposure

Pre-settlement risk isn't measured trade by trade in practice. It starts with a market-consistent discount curve, and it's monitored at the netting-set level across a counterparty's full portfolio, not per instrument.

USD Yield Curve Bootstrap
Strips today's zero curve and discount factors out of observable market quotes: O/N through 6M deposits, 1Y to 10Y par swap rates. Sequential bootstrap with log-linear interpolation for coupon dates that aren't directly quoted. Every exposure model below discounts off this curve instead of an assumed rate.
10 market quotes → full curve Short end: 4.25% 5Y trough: 3.46% 10Y: 3.64%
DOWNLOAD .PY DOWNLOAD CHART
FX Forward PFE/EE + Netting Set Aggregation vs. the IRS
1Y USD/JPY forward priced off the bootstrapped curve via covered interest parity, simulated jointly and correlated (ρ=-0.25) with the swap's rate paths. The exposure shape is the opposite of the swap's, climbing steadily into a single cash flow at maturity instead of humping and decaying. Then nets both trades under one counterparty CSA against just summing them.
Gross PFE 95%: $4.10M Netted PFE 95%: $3.47M Netting benefit: 15.4% 50,000 correlated paths
DOWNLOAD .PY DOWNLOAD CHART

The bootstrapped curve values are replicated as constants in the FX forward script so it runs standalone. In a real setup these would share a single curve object instead of being duplicated.

Independent Price Verification

Not every model here is exposure-side risk. This one sits closer to model validation, checking a closed-form pricing shortcut against full simulation before trusting it.

Monte Carlo Verification: Big Six Bank Basket Structured Note
A principal-at-risk note on an equal-weight basket of the six major Canadian banks, 3 year tenor. Basket call (K=100) and put (K=90) priced with a closed-form approximation, then checked against 1,000,000 correlated Monte Carlo paths. Both prices land inside the simulation's 95% confidence interval, so the approximation holds up.
Call: 8.50 (CF) vs 8.49±0.03 (MC) Put: 3.76 (CF) vs 3.76±0.01 (MC) Both verified in 95% CI P(90 buffer breach): 34.6%
DOWNLOAD .PY DOWNLOAD CHART

Generative & ML Models

A different piece of the toolkit. This learns an empirical distribution from data instead of assuming a closed-form one, which earns its keep when the target is messy and behavioral, like mortgage prepayment, driven by actual borrowers making refinancing decisions. It's not a general substitute for the pricing models above.

Conditional Prepayment Rate Modeling: Log-GAN vs XGBoost Quantile vs WGAN-GP
Three approaches to modeling mortgage CPR on Fannie Mae 15 year fixed benchmark pools, which is zero-inflated and right-skewed: a log-transformed conditional GAN, gradient-boosted quantile regression, and a Wasserstein GAN with gradient penalty. All hand-implemented, forward and backward passes and Adam from scratch, no autodiff. Checked with KS tests against the real distribution instead of eyeballing it. Along the way I found a real bug: the discriminator training called forward() twice before backward(), which silently overwrote the cached activations and was making the WGAN-GP diverge. Fixed it by combining the real and fake batches into a single forward/backward pass, and added gradient clipping since the gradient penalty here is monitored rather than backpropagated into the critic.
Log-GAN D-loss converges to ~1.29 (theory: ln4≈1.39) 3,327 pool cohorts Top driver: pre-winter CPR Hand-rolled MLP, no autodiff
DOWNLOAD .PY DOWNLOAD CHART
Investment Philosophy

Investment Process

I invest in businesses rather than market narratives. My objective is to own high-quality companies that can compound intrinsic value over long periods while minimizing the risk of permanent capital loss.

Rather than forecasting macroeconomic cycles or short-term market movements, I focus on understanding how a business creates value, whether its competitive advantage is durable, and whether the current market price offers a meaningful margin of safety.

What I Look For

Every investment must demonstrate durable business quality supported by measurable fundamentals.

I prioritize companies with:

  • High and sustainable returns on invested capital (ROIC)
  • Strong free cash flow generation
  • Pricing power and resilient margins
  • Asset-light or capital-efficient business models
  • Disciplined capital allocation
  • Long-term reinvestment opportunities

Research Process

Investment ideas originate from a repeatable, bottom-up screening process.

My workflow follows five steps:

  1. Screen for financially strong businesses using quantitative filters.
  2. Evaluate competitive advantages, industry structure, and management quality.
  3. Estimate intrinsic value through conservative valuation and scenario analysis.
  4. Allocate capital based on conviction, valuation, and downside protection.
  5. Continuously monitor fundamentals and reassess the investment thesis.

Portfolio Construction

Not every investment deserves the same amount of capital.

Position sizing reflects business quality, valuation, conviction, and expected long-term risk-adjusted returns. Core holdings receive the largest allocations, while new ideas begin as smaller positions and earn additional capital only as conviction increases.

Sell Discipline

Every position continuously competes for capital.

I sell or reduce a position only when:

  • The thesis changes. The original investment case is no longer valid.
  • Valuation exceeds intrinsic value. Expected future returns no longer justify the risk.
  • A superior opportunity exists. Capital can be redeployed into a more attractive investment.

What I Avoid

I generally avoid businesses with:

  • Excessive leverage
  • Commodity-driven earnings
  • Weak pricing power
  • Structurally declining industries
  • Speculative narratives unsupported by free cash flow
  • Valuations that rely on overly optimistic assumptions
